title: Quadrupole Non-Selective Pulse Width Optimization
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"1h78s","text":"Purpose","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"276p6","text":"Calibration of non-selective 90° pulse width using a standard sample for a quadrupolar nucleus.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2sss7","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dd1kr","text":"Scope","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3hmf4","text":"This protocol uses a standard sample with high sensitivity and low quadrupolar coupling constant. This can be a solid sample with negligible CQ (e.g., NaCl for 23Na) or a solution sample (e.g., 1 M NaCl for 23Na).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":141,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":145,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":188,"length":3},{"style":"sub","offset":142,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":160,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":207,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"27dce","text":"This SOP should be among the first executed when investigating a new sample. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"emaag","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"62n6e","text":"This SOP is written to the level of an experienced NMR spectroscopist who is unfamiliar with the acquisition of spectra from quadrupolar nuclei.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1fh0p","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f9a8a","text":"Calibration and validation of a central transition-selective pulse is outside the scope of this SOP; CT-selective pulses should be calibrated on the sample of interest using SOP Materials Central-Transition-Selective Pulse Width Optimization.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"1pott","text":"This protocol is intended for a high-symmetry standard sample with low CQ, such as NaCl for 23Na. A 1 M solution of an appropriate salt can also be used.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":71,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":72,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":92,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d7862","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8kuo8","text":"Probe must be well-tuned and well-matched.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4tkp8","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fnjv9","text":"SOP written for Bruker spectrometers.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3qfo1","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9ghcq","text":"Expected completion time: \u003c1 hour ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"2qqh5","text":"User should be familiar with the power limitations and duty cycle of the probe being used.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"feo0o","text":"Definitions:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f92k4","text":"CT: Central Transition","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2p6u1","text":"CQ: Quadrupolar coupling constant","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":1,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3o2ba","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dq2m0","text":"Appendix:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"35hog","text":"The 360° zero crossing is less reliable in half-integer quadrupoles than in spin-1/2 nuclei. The 180° zero crossing point is preferred.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"apblq","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4sodr","text":"The nutation curve of will be non-sinusoidal if the relaxation delay d1 is too short; however, the 180° zero crossing should be reliable regardless of precise relaxation time. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":69,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"718u0","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ovo1","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Open a new experiment window and load the zg pulse sequence.
Acquire an initial spectrum and ensure that there is adequate signal. If signal is inadequate, increase the number of scans and repeat this step.
Array pulse width p1 from 1.0 μs to 8 μs in steps of 0.5 μs. There should be 15 experiments.
Run the popt and observe the nutation curve.
Determine the 90° pulse length by finding the pulse length corresponding to the 180° zero crossing point and dividing it by 2.
If a more accurate determination is required, popt can be run again with revised start, end, and step values.
If a particular radiofrequency power is desired, use the pulse au program to estimate the amplifier power required and optimize the pulse power plw1 using popt .
Confirm the pulse power limitations of the probe prior to optimizing plw1 and ensure that the optimization routine will not allow for the power limit to be exceeded.

Load previously known good pulse length p1 and pulse power plw1 . If previously known good values aren't available, a reasonable starting point is a 1 μs pulse using a power level from a nearby nucleus, e.g. 13 C and 23 Na. If there is no information at all on previously known pulse lengths and powers in this frequency range, consult the local expert responsible for the probe being used. If you are that expert and need guidance, consult with the probe vendor.
Repeat this protocol until the pulse length and power are optimized to the level required.
creator: NMRFAM Facility
